result:
The first thing that comes to me is the silence. Not a true silence, of course—there’s the hum of a distant lawnmower, the sigh of the air conditioner, the faint, metallic taste of static from a screen left on too long. But the inner silence, the one we’re supposed to covet, that’s what’s loud today. It’s a blank page silence. And what comes to fill it is a tide of fragments.

I think about the last time I was truly, unreasonably happy. It was last month, on a Tuesday. Nothing special. I was walking home and the light hit a puddle in a particular way, turning it into a perfect, shimmering disk of gasoline rainbow, a tiny portal to another dimension right there on the asphalt. And for a moment, I was eight years old again, convinced I’d discovered magic. The adult part of me, the one that pays taxes and worries about retirement, watched this happen with a sort of tender amusement. Then we moved on. But that fragment sticks. It’s a pebble in the shoe of my memory.

Then, of course, comes the worry. It’s less a thought and more a weather system that rolls in. Will the people I love be safe today? Is that strange noise the car makes something catastrophic? Am I doing enough, being enough, feeling enough? The modern mind is a magnificent machine for generating problems that don’t exist yet. We’ve traded sabre-tooth tigers for spreadsheet errors, but the adrenal response is identical. We are haunted by futures we are endlessly composing and revising.

And memory—memory is not a filing cabinet. It’s a chef. It takes the raw ingredients of what happened, chops them up, seasons them with how we felt later, and serves us a reconstructed meal, presented as the original. The taste is never quite right, but we swear it’s authentic. I remember my grandmother’s kitchen smelling of yeast and cinnamon. But was it always? Or just on the best days? I have decided to keep the cinnamon. The truth is less important than the warmth it gives me.

This makes me think of connections. The invisible threads. The man who delivered the mail this morning has a life as vast and complex as my own, with its own beloved puddles and private worries. The barista who made my coffee is an amateur botanist. The novelist whose book I’m reading is, at this very second, perhaps staring at her own blank page, wrestling with her own tide of fragments. We are all simultaneously the main character and an extra in everyone else’s story. We are all walking anthologies of a story only we have read.

Sometimes, what comes to me is a deep, almost gravitational pull towards smallness. Not insignificance, but the smallness of detail. The geometry of a spiderweb beaded with dew. The precise click of a keyboard key. The way a loved one’s handwriting slopes across an envelope. In an age of grand narratives and shouting headlines, salvation might lie in this focused attention. To look so closely at one thing that the world around it dissolves. It’s a kind of voluntary, beautiful myopia.

Other times, it’s the opposite—a vertiginous sense of scale. We are on a wet rock spinning through a void, heated by a nuclear furnace 93 million miles away, orbiting a supermassive black hole at the center of a galaxy we call “Milky” like a child naming a kitten. And on this rock, we’ve built pyramids and symphonies, formulated laws of physics and then broken them, composed love letters and declarations of war. The absurdity is breathtaking. It should make us nihilistic, but instead, it often makes us kind. Or try to be. What else can you do, faced with such a magnificent, accidental gift?

Then there is the recurring thought about time. We imagine it as a river, flowing in one direction. But what if it’s more like an ocean? The past is not behind us; it’s sunk beneath the surface, but the water is still here, part of the same medium. The future is just a different part of the same vast body, yet to be disturbed by our wake. We are only ever in the present, the fragile interface between air and water, light and depth. And our consciousness is the temporary foam on that ever-changing surface.

So what am I to do with these 1000 words? This tiny vessel? I could build an argument, teach a lesson, spin a tale. But perhaps the most honest thing is to simply show the process. To say: this is what a mind does when set loose with a quota of words. It meanders. It worries. It marvels. It connects disparate dots into a shaky constellation and calls it insight.

The lawnmower has stopped. The silence changes shape again. The fragments begin to settle, like snow in a globe that’s been shaken.

And what remains, when the noise dies down? A simple, unoriginal, but persistent feeling: a desire to be gentle. With myself, for the cacophony inside. With others, for the cacophony I cannot hear but know is there. With this world, which is brutal and beautiful in equal, incomprehensible measure.

The page is no longer blank. It is a map of a few minutes of human consciousness. It is messy, contradictory, and deeply unfinished. But it is here. And for now, that feels like enough. It is a record of a moment saying to itself, “I am.” And then, because the words are almost used up and the silence is returning, it adds, in a whisper that is both question and answer: “Aren’t we all?”

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
